resources.string: PhotoSync only works on your home Wi‑Fi — the same network your computer is on. Tap \"Find server\" while at home.
API key (leave empty if not set)
Searching for servers on this WiFi…

Try this:

(a) Make sure the PhotoSync Server app is running on your PC.
(b) Check that both devices are on the same WiFi network.
(c) Or type the address shown in the PC app's Settings into the field above.

Haven't installed it on your computer yet? Tap "Set it up" below.
Set it up on the computer
This server requires an API key — enter it above.
Enter or discover a server URL first
Sync started (runs when WiFi is available)
Auto-sync enabled — runs every 15 min on WiFi
PhotoSync needs photo access to back up your pictures
I\'m using PhotoSync to back up my phone\'s photos to my own computer — free, private, no cloud subscription. You can set it up here:\n\n%1$s

Your photos back up to a small free app — PhotoSync Server — that runs on your home computer (Windows). You only set it up once, and it then starts on its own whenever the computer is on.\n\nThe easiest way: send yourself the setup link, then open it on your computer and tap Download for Windows.
Send the setup link to your computer
Or type this address on your computer:
Already installed it on the computer? Go back and tap Find server while on your home Wi‑Fi.
No photos found on this device
Done — your photos back up automatically
PhotoSync is backing them up to your own computer — no cloud, no subscription. Know someone tired of paying for photo storage?
Tells you when a newer version of PhotoSync is available.
Version %1$s is available — tap to update.
Can\'t reach your server — check it\'s on and the address is right in Settings
Set up your server in Settings first
Make sure you\'re on your home Wi‑Fi and the PhotoSync Server app is running on your PC.
No photos stored on the server yet
Set up your server in Settings first
The selected photo(s) will be removed from this phone. Your server backup will not be affected.
The selected photo(s) will be permanently deleted from this phone AND from your server. This cannot be undone.
Deleted from device. Could not reach server: %1$s
Your photos are backed up to their own folder on the server, under this name. Each person in your household can use their own name.
🔒 Your photos go only to your own computer at home — nothing is uploaded to the internet or any company.
Enter your name in Settings first

Your photos will be saved to a folder named \"%1$s\" on the server.\n\nIf you share the server with others (family, housemates), make sure nobody else is already using this name — there\'s no automatic check for duplicates.
Photos and videos taken with your camera (the DCIM folder). Recommended.
Every photo and video folder on this device.
Tap folders to include in your backup:
Your folders (choose \"Selected folders\" above to pick):
No folders found on this device

PhotoSync backs up your phone\'s photos and videos straight to your family\'s own computer at home — not to a company\'s cloud. That\'s what keeps it private and free.\n\nBecause the photos go to that computer, your phone can only back them up when both are on the same home Wi‑Fi and the computer is turned on.
Why does it only work on my home Wi‑Fi?

Your photos are saved on your own computer at home, not on the internet. For now PhotoSync backs up only while your phone and that computer share the same home Wi‑Fi — it\'s the simplest and safest way to reach it, and since most people are home every day your photos still get backed up regularly. On mobile data or a different Wi‑Fi — work, a café, a friend\'s house — backups simply pause and pick up again automatically once you\'re home. (Backing up from anywhere may come in a future version.)
Why do I need to add a server?

Unlike Google Photos, no company is storing your pictures. The \"server\" is just the free PhotoSync Server app running on your household\'s computer — that\'s where your photos are kept. The app on your phone needs its address so it knows where to send them. Tap \"Find server\" in Settings while at home and it\'s usually detected automatically; if not, open the PhotoSync Server app on your computer — it shows the address you can type in.
Are my photos uploaded to the internet?
No. They go only to your own computer on your home network and stay on its drive. Nothing is sent to the cloud or to us.
Do I need to keep the app open, and why isn\'t it instant?

No — the app doesn't need to stay open. Once set up, it backs up on its own in the background while you're on home Wi‑Fi, and you can tap "Sync now" anytime from the menu. (The PhotoSync Server on your computer does need to be running, but by default it starts with the computer and runs quietly, so as long as the computer is on, backups keep working.)

It isn't always instant, though. To save battery, Android limits what apps can do in the background, so a new photo can take a while to upload — longer if the phone has been sitting locked and untouched. Nothing is ever lost; it always catches up.

To make backups happen sooner: plug the phone in to charge (Android lifts its background limits while charging, so this is when most backups happen — like overnight), stay on your home Wi‑Fi and make sure it isn't set to "Metered", and open PhotoSync any time you want it to back up immediately.
Does it back up while my phone is locked?

Yes. While it\'s uploading you\'ll see a \"Backing up…\" notification, and it keeps going with the screen off as long as you\'re on home Wi‑Fi.\n\nIf backups seem to stop when the phone is locked or idle, your phone\'s battery saver may be putting the app to sleep. To prevent that, open Android Settings ▸ Apps ▸ PhotoSync ▸ Battery and set it to Unrestricted. (Some phones — Samsung, Xiaomi, OnePlus and others — are especially aggressive about this.)
Why haven\'t my latest photos backed up yet?
Check that you\'re on your home Wi‑Fi and the computer is on. Backups run periodically and resume on their own — pull down on the gallery to refresh, or use \"Sync now\".
Can I delete a photo from both my phone and the server at once?

Yes — long-press any photo in the gallery to enter selection mode, then tap Trash. This removes it from your phone and tells the server to delete its copy too.\n\nThe Trash button requires an active connection to your server (same home Wi‑Fi, server app running). If the server is unreachable the button will be grayed out — in that case you can still use Delete from device to remove the local copy, but the server copy will remain. There is currently no automatic retry, so if you delete while offline the server copy will not be cleaned up later.
Can I add photos to the server manually?

Yes. PhotoSync Server stores photos as plain files on your PC, organised in folders by user and date — for example PhotoSync\Alex\2026\06\photo.jpg. You can copy any photo or video into that folder structure yourself and the server will serve it normally.

The one extra step: the server keeps an index of known files to avoid duplicates during upload. Files copied in manually aren't in that index yet, so they won't appear in "Photos on server" until you scan for them. To do that, open the PhotoSync Server app on your PC, go to Settings, and click Scan for new files. The scan is instant and safe to run repeatedly — it only adds what isn't already indexed.

Folder layout: create a subfolder named after the person (Alex, Maria…) inside the backup folder, then year and month subfolders inside that. Example: PhotoSync\Alex\2026\06\IMG_1234.jpg. If you skip the person's name folder, files land under the default account instead.

Yes. PhotoSync is free and open source, and because the storage is your own computer there\'s no subscription and no limit beyond your own drive. It\'s built by one person on evenings and weekends — if it\'s useful to you, a small tip helps keep it going (entirely optional).

I\'m a father, engineer, and product builder. I started PhotoSync because photo storage has become expensive and subscription-heavy — yet external drives are cheap and most people already own a computer at home. I wanted a simpler alternative that gives people control over their own photos.\n\nIt\'s developed in my evenings and weekends. If you find it valuable, you can buy me a coffee — every contribution helps me spend more time building and improving the app.
